Text-EQ:
We consider the projection onto the $\ell_{1, \infty}$ norm ball for solving group sparse optimization problems arising in multi-task learning. 
Based on the primal-dual gradient method (PDG), we present a novel primal-dual Newton method, which updates the primal and dual iterates incrementally with Newton steps. 
We exploit the problem-inherent structure so that the closed-form for the primal-dual Newton steps can be derived easily. 
Compared with existing algorithms, our approach does not need to maintain the primal feasibility, nor require the computation of the $\ell_1$ ball projection subproblems. 
We prove that our algorithm terminates after a finite number of iterations. 
Numerical simulations on synthetic and real-world data show that our proposed method is faster than the previous state-of-the-art.

Optimization problems involve group-sparse appear in a variety of applications and are popular in engineering, statistics and many other fields.
For example, it is widely applied in machine learning for improving the stability of the feature selection \cite{Sra11,Sra12} and solving collective face recognition problems   \cite{Wang17}, resulting in various mixed-norm constrained regression methods  \cite{Kowal09b}. 
It is also used for signal denoising \cite{Kowal09a} and simultaneous sparse
signal approximation \cite{tropp2006algorithms} in signal processing. 
To promote group-sparse structure in the variables of interest, 
the $\ell_{1, p}$ norm ($p \geq 2$) constraint has been shown to be useful. 
Generally, large $p$ indicates strong correlation among the groups of the variables  \cite{Obozi10}.
In particular, the $\ell_{1, \infty}$ norm ball constrained optimization problems have  attracted numerous attentions in many applications \cite{Bejar19, gustavo2018fast, Chau19} such as image annotation \cite{Quat09}, multi-task learning \cite{Liu09} and multitask lasso regression  \cite{Sra11,Sra12}.
In this paper, we consider solving the $\ell_{1, \infty}$ norm ball constrained optimization problems of the form:

where $f:\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}\to\mathbb{R}$ is a convex differentiable loss functions measures the error of $\mathbf{W}$. $C>0$ is a constant that controls the group sparsity which can be user-specified. 
The other commonly seen formulation is to penalize the $\ell_{1,\infty}$ norm of $\mathbf{W}$ into the objective. 
Compared with the penalized version, the constrained version here  \eqref{problem:opt_on_ball} is usually more favorable since after selecting the value of $C$, one can know the group sparsity of the solutions prior to solving the problem.

where $\gamma^k>0$ is the stepsize and $\mathcal{P}_{\ell_{1,\infty},C}(\cdot)$ is the Euclidean projection onto the $\ell_{1,\infty}$ ball with radius $C$ that will be defined in the next section formally.

GPA enjoys fast convergence rate \cite{Berts76} compared with  many other first-order  projection-free methods such as 
the Frank-Wolfe method \cite{frank1956algorithm, Luss13} (\emph{a.k.a.} conditional gradient method), 
and can incorporate many techniques such as 
the spectral step size \cite{Barzi88} and non-monotone line search \cite{Gripp86}  to improve the performance. 
At each iteration, GPA moves along the negative gradient and then projects the results point to satisfy the constraints as stated in \eqref{eq:projection_step}.
Therefore, its performance critically depends on the efficiency of projection operation and may suffer from heavy computational burden if the projection subproblem is difficult to solve. 
Therefore, our primary focus in this paper is on designing a fast projection method onto the $\ell_{1, \infty}$ norm ball.

To find the projection onto the $\ell_{1, \infty}$ norm ball, several methods have appeared in the past decade. 
A common approach is to reformulate the dual problem of the projection as a univariate equation, and then using a root-finding technique to determine the optimal dual solution. 
\cite{Quat09} proposed a sorting-based algorithm (SBM) to find the root of a piece-wise linear function with $mn+1$ breakpoints, which is shown to have the worst case complexity of $\mathcal{O}(mn\log(mn))$ . 
\cite{Sra11} studied the projection onto the $ \ell_{1, q}$ $(q > 1)$ norm ball and converted the projection problem to a root-finding problem, which can then be attacked by the bisection method.

The dual method was then outperformed by the primal-dual methods, for example, the dual ascent (DA) method. 
DA constructs the Lagrangian of the projection problem; at each iteration, it finds the exact solution to the primal subproblem for a fixed dual variable and then updates the dual variable by a gradient ascent step.
\cite{gustavo2018fast} first uses DA to solve the $\ell_{1,\infty}$ norm ball projection problem by updating the dual variable with the Steffensen root search method. 
\cite{Chau19} then improved the Steffensen method with the Newton root search method (NRFM), which achieves the best performance among all root-finding-based methods. 
\cite{Bejar19} proposed a general iterative algorithm for computing the proximal operator of the $\ell_{1,\infty}$ norm and proved that the algorithm converges in finite steps.
The DA relies on computing the optimal solution to the $\ell_1$ norm ball projection subproblem, which may become a computational burden if the dimension becomes large.

The most recent work \cite{pmlr-v119-chu20b} proposed a semi-smooth Newton algorithm (SNA) by solving the KKT equations directly.
The use of the second-order information enables it to become the state-of-the-art solver.
Moreover, a local $Q$-superlinearly convergence rate or the finite step convergence of SNA is established.

In this paper, we proposed a primal-dual Newton method for solving the $\ell_{1,\infty}$ norm ball projection, which is based on the well-known primal-dual gradient (PDG) method for solving convex constrained optimization problems. 
 Different from DA, the PDG updates the primal variable by gradient descent steps and the dual variable by gradient ascent steps incrementally. 
Compared with the DA, the PDG can avoid repeatedly solving multiple $\ell_1$ norm ball projection subproblems in our case, 
and attains a linear convergence rate since the problem is strongly convex and strongly smooth. \cite{du2019linear, alghunaim2020linear}.

Moreover, our proposed algorithm is more robust to initialization of the dual variable compared to SNA, since SNA has the restrictions on the primal feasibility, and an improper initial guess may causes divergence of the algorithm.
Table 1 gives a qualitative comparison of existing methods for computing the projection onto the $\ell_{1,\infty}$ norm ball with respect to different algorithmic properties.

Let $\mathbb{R}^n$ be the $n$-dimensional Euclidean space and $\mathbb{R}_+$ be the nonnegative orthant.  
For any $ \bm{x}\in \mathbb{R}^n$ and $p\geq 1$, 
we denote the $\ell_p$ norm as $\|\bm{x}\|_p = (\sum_{i=1}^n|x_i|^p)^{1/p}$; the infinity norm is defined as $\|\bm{x}\|_\infty = \max\{|x_1|,\ldots,|x_n|\}$.  
We use $[x]_+=\max(x,0)$ and denote $[n]=\{1,\dots,n\}$ for simplicity. 
We represent a matrix  $\mathbf{A}=(A_{ij})\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ with its rows: $\mathbf{A}=\left[\bm{a}_1;\dots;\bm{a}_m\right]$, where $\bm{a}_i\in\mathbb{R}^{1\times n}$ is the $i$-th row of $\mathbf{A}$, so $\bm{a}_{ij}=A_{ij}$, we will use two kinds of representation alternatively in this paper for convenience.
For any $\mathbf{A},\mathbf{B}\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$, the absolute value of $\mathbf{A}$ is define as $|\mathbf{A}| = (|A_{ij}|)$, the Hadamard product between $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{B}$ is defined as $\mathbf{A} \odot \mathbf{B} = (A_{ij} B_{ij})$. 
We also define $\mathcal{I}(\mu_i)$ as the index of elements of $i$-th row of $\mathbf{A}$ that is greater than or equal to $\mu_i$\cite{pmlr-v119-chu20b}, that is, $\mathcal{I}(\mu_i)=\{j|A_{ij}\geq\mu_i\}$.
Let $|S|$ be the cardinality of a given index set $S$.
The Frobenuis norm of $\mathbf{A}$ is given by $\|\mathbf{A}\|_F=\left(\sum_{i=1}^m\sum_{j=1}^nA_{ij}^2\right)^{1/2}$, and the $\ell_{1,\infty}$ norm  of $\mathbf{A}$ is defined as

It can be seen that the $\ell_{1,\infty}$ norm is determined by the largest absolute value of each row. 
If $\|\bm{a}_i\|_{\infty}=0$, the corresponding row of $\mathbf{A}$ is also zero.
If $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ is a convex function, then the subdifferential of $f$ at point $ \bm{\bar x} $ is given by
$
\partial f(\bm{\bar x}) := \{ \bm{z} \mid f(\bm{\bar x}) + \left\langle \bm{z},\, \bm{x} - \bm{\bar x} \right\rangle \leq f(\bm{x}), \ \forall \ \bm{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n \}.
$  
An element in $\partial f(\bar{\bm{x}})$ is called the subgradient of $f$ at $\bar{\bm{x}}$. 
In particular, if $f$ is differentiable, then $\partial f(\bar{\bm{x}}) = \{\nabla f(\bar{\bm{x}})\}$.
Given a matrix $\mathbf{A}\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$, we focus on computing the projection of $\mathbf{A}$ onto the $\ell_{1,\infty}$ ball:
where $C>0$ is the radius of the $\ell_{1,\infty}$ ball.

To simplify problem \eqref{problem:original_problem}, we first introduce the following lemma, which restricts the sign of the entries of $\mathbf{A}$ to be non-negative, the proof is omitted here and we refer to \cite{Bejar19, duchi2008efficient} for interested readers.
The sign of the optimal solution $\mathbf{W}^*$ of problem \eqref{problem:original_problem} must match the sign of $\mathbf{A}$, that is, $W_{ij}^*A_{ij}\geq0$, $\forall (i,j)\in[m]\times[n]$.

We can now assume that $\mathbf{A}\in\mathbb{R}_+^{m\times n}$ since otherwise by Lemma \ref{lemma:matched_sign} the optimal solution can be obtained by first computing the projection of $|\mathbf{A}|$ onto the $\ell_{1,\infty}$ ball and then multiplying it element-wise by $sign(\mathbf{A})$.
Moreover, note that if $\|\mathbf{A}\|_{1,\infty}\leq C $, then the solution to \eqref{problem:original_problem} is simply $\mathbf{A}$ itself, 
so we also assume that $\|\mathbf{A}\|_{1,\infty}> C$ in this paper.

The dimension of problem \eqref{problem:original_problem} is $mn$, which can be reduced to $m$. Using the property of $\ell_{1,\infty}$ norm, we first introduce the 
slack variable $\mu_i=\|\bm{w}_i\|_{\infty}$ for  $i=1,\dots,m$, and rewrite problem \eqref{problem:original_problem} as
If we fix $\boldsymbol\mu$, the problem above is separable to $\bm{w}_i$:

where $\mathbf{a}\preceq \bm{b}$ means  $\bm{b}-\mathbf{a}\in\mathbb{R}_+$. 
Note that problem \eqref{problem:4} is the projection of $\bm{a}_i$ onto the box constraint, which has a closed-form solution \cite{beck}:
Substituting \eqref{eq:opt_w} back into problem \eqref{problem:5}, we obtain an equivalent optimization problem with dimension $m$:
A point $(\boldsymbol\mu^*,\lambda^*)\in\mathbb{R}_+^m\times\mathbb{R}$ is a \emph{saddle point} of problem \eqref{problem:saddle_point_problem} if

If $(\boldsymbol\mu^*,\lambda^*)$ is a saddle point of problem \eqref{problem:saddle_point_problem}, 
then $\boldsymbol\mu^*$ is the optimal solution to problem \eqref{eq:opt_on_mu} \cite{Rockafellar2015}. 
We first introduce the optimality conditions for saddle point problem  \eqref{problem:saddle_point_problem}:
The saddle point $(\boldsymbol\mu^*, \lambda^*)$ of problem \eqref{problem:saddle_point_problem} satisfies the following optimality conditions:

Now our goal is to solve problem \eqref{problem:saddle_point_problem}.
Once the saddle point $(\boldsymbol\mu^*,\lambda^*)$ is found, the following lemma can be used to retrieve the optimal solution to problem \eqref{problem:original_problem}.

Let $(\boldsymbol\mu^*,\lambda^*)$ be the saddle point to problem \eqref{problem:saddle_point_problem}. Then the optimal solution $\mathbf{W}^*$ to \eqref{problem:original_problem} satisfies the following equation:
where $\mathcal{B}^* := \{i\mid \|\bm{a}_i\|_1>\lambda^*\}$.

We first define $\mu_i(\lambda^k)$ as the exact solution of the primal update at the $k$-th iteration for given $\lambda^k$, meaning 
it satisfies

It be seen from the optimal condition \eqref{eq:primal_stationary} and the fact that $\nabla_i\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol\mu,\lambda)$ is a monotone function of $\mu_i$, we have $\mu_i^*=\mu_i(\lambda^*)$.

Due to the fact that $\nabla_i\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol\mu^k,\lambda^{k})$ is a piece-wise linear function with respect to $\mu_i$ with at most $n$ breakpoints, 
inspired by \cite{Rodriguez,michelot1986finite}, the primal update in this paper takes the form:

where $\beta_i^k=1/|\mathcal{I}(\mu_i^k)|$ and $\mathcal{B}(\lambda) := \{i\mid \|\bm{a}_i\|_1>\lambda\}$.
We also use $\mathcal{B}^k$ as shorthand for $\mathcal{B}(\lambda^k)$.
Since $\nabla_i\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol\mu^k,\lambda^{k})$ is convex with respect to $\mu_i$ and $-1/\beta_i^k\in\partial \nabla_i\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol\mu^k,\lambda^{k})$, \eqref{eq:primal_update} is therefore a Newton update, 
meaning that the next iterate  $\mu_i^{k+1}$ is
determined by the intersection of the tangent line of $\nabla_i\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol\mu^k,\lambda^{k})$ at the point $\boldsymbol\mu^k$ and the $\mu$ axis. 
We depict this updating strategy in Fig \ref{fig:primal_update}.
If $\mu_i^k\leq \|\bm{a}_i\|_\infty$ and $\lambda^k>0$, then the update \eqref{eq:primal_update} is well-defined.

Notice that in update \eqref{eq:primal_update}, it does not necessarily hold $\mu_i^{k+1}=\mu_i(\lambda^k)$ for a given $\lambda^k$.
We do not use $\mu_i(\lambda^k)$ as our next iteration, 
since it needs to solve the piece-wise linear equation \eqref{eq:exact_primal}, 
which has a worst complexity of $O(n^2)$ \cite{michelot1986finite}. 
This represents the main difference from DA, 
since DA involves computing $\mu_i(\lambda^k)$ at each iteration for a given $\lambda^k$. 
We summarize this in the following lemma.

For $i\in[m]$, $\mu_i^k\leq\|\bm{a}_i\|_\infty$. 
    If $\beta_i^k=1/|\mathcal{I}(\mu_i^k)|$, then $\mu_i^{k+1}\leq\mu_i(\lambda^k)$. 
    Moreover, if $\lambda^{k+n+1}=\cdots=\lambda^k$, meaning $\lambda$ is unchanged over iterations $k,\dots,k+n+1$, then there exists $j\in[n]$ such that $\mu_i^{k+n+1}=\cdots=\mu_i^{k+j}=\mu_i(\lambda^k)$.

Let $h(\mu):=\sum_{i=1}^m[A_{ij}-\mu]_+-\lambda^k$, which is a convex, piece-wise linear and non-increasing function.
    Let $\pi(\mu):=h(\mu_i^k)+h'(\mu_i^k)(\mu-\mu_i^k)$, where $h'(\mu_i^k)=-|\mathcal{I}(\mu_i^k)|\in\partial h(\mu_i^k)$. 
    It holds that $h(\mu_i^{k+1})\geq\pi(\mu_i^{k+1})=0$.
    Therefore, $\mu_i^{k+1}\leq \mu_i(\lambda^k)$ by the fact that $h(\mu)$ is monotonically non-increasing.

Let $\mu_i^k\in[\theta_1,\theta_2)$, where $\theta_1<\theta_2$ are two consecutive breakpoints of $h(\mu)$.
    Notice that $h$ and $\pi$ have the same slope $h'(\mu_i^k)$ on $[\theta_1,\theta_2)$ and $h(\mu_i^k)=\pi(\mu_i^k)$, hence $\pi(\mu)=h(\mu)$ for $\mu\in[\theta_1,\theta_2)$
    If $\mu_i(\lambda^k)\in[\theta_1,\theta_2]$, we have $\pi(\mu(\lambda^k))=0$ and $\mu_i^{k+1}=\mu(\lambda^k)$; if $\mu_i(\lambda^k)>\theta_2$, we have $\pi(\theta_2)=h(\theta_2)>0$. So $\mu_i^{k+1}>\theta_2$. Since there are at most $n+1$ breakpoints, we must have $\mu_i^{k+n+1}=\cdots=\mu_i^{k+j}=\mu_i(\lambda^k)$ for some $j\in[n]$.

The above Lemma reveals that $\mu_i^{k+1}$ is always an underestimate of $\mu_i(\lambda^k)$.
In the worst case, if the dual variable remains unchanged, then we obtain $\mu_i(\lambda^k)$ in at most $n$ steps. 
If this happens, then our algorithm behaves the same as NRFM proposed by \cite{Chau19} for finding the new iterate $\mu_i^{k+1}$.

Typically, $\alpha_k$ is chosen as some constant related to the strongly convex parameter \cite{du2019linear, alghunaim2020linear}.
To achieve faster convergence, after obtaining $\boldsymbol\mu^{k+1}$, we do not adopt this kind of updating strategy for $\lambda$. 
Since $\nabla_\lambda\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol\mu^{k+1},\lambda)$ is Lipschitz continuous with respect to $\lambda$, $\nabla_{\lambda\lambda}\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol\mu^k,\lambda)$ exists almost everywhere \cite{federer2014geometric}, thus we can invoke semismooth Newton methods to update $\lambda^k$ , which takes the following form:

where $\nabla_{\lambda\lambda}\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol\mu^k,\lambda^{k})$ is a general Hessian \cite{7040304}. 
Note that $\nabla_{\lambda}\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol\mu^{k+1},\lambda^k)= \sum_{i\in\mathcal{B}^k}\mu_i^{k+1}-C$, we take the derivative of $\mu_i^{k+1}$ with respect to $\lambda^k$ and obtain the Newton step for obtaining $\lambda^{k+1}$:

which means a Newton step can be obtained by setting $\alpha^k=1/\sum_{i\in\mathcal{B}^k}\beta_i^{k+1}$. 
By the definition of $\mathcal{B}^k$, $\mathcal{I}(\mu_i^{k+1})\neq\emptyset$ always holds, so that the stepsize $\alpha^k$ is always and well-defined.
Compared with the original update, we add $[\cdot]_+$ to create a monotonically increasing dual variable sequence.

Though we set the initialization in the Algorithm \ref{alg:PDN} as $\boldsymbol\mu^0=\mathbf{0}_{m\times 1}$. It should be emphasized that our initialization of $\boldsymbol\mu^0$ does not depend on $\lambda^0$ as SNA, which requires that $\mu_i^0=\mu_i(\lambda^0)$ or it will suffer from the slowly convergence rate even the diverge problems.

Moreover, we can see that the memory space needed by our
algorithm is less than that of SNA, since we only have to
save $\boldsymbol\mu^k$, whereas sna needs to save $\boldsymbol\mu^{k}$ and $\boldsymbol\mu^{k+1}$ simultaneously. our algorithm is capable of saving the memory space by updating $\boldsymbol\mu^k$ in place. This is important in large-scale problems since stores $\boldsymbol\mu^k$ may be also expensive.

Let  $s(\lambda):=\sum_{i\in\mathcal{B}^k}\mu_i(\lambda)-C$ where $\mu_i(\lambda)$ is defined in \eqref{eq:exact_primal}. It can be seen that $s(\lambda)$ is a monotone decreasing function with 
$ s'(\lambda):=\sum_{i\in\mathcal{B}^k}1/|\mathcal{I}(\mu_i(\lambda))|\in \partial s(\lambda)$. We will use this property in the proof of convergence analysis.
The range of the optimal dual variable $\lambda^*$ can be determined as follows.

The optimal dual variable $\lambda^*$ defined in Lemma \ref{lemma:KKT_conditin} satisfies $\lambda^*\in[\lambda_l,\lambda_r)$, where $\lambda_l=(\sum_{i=1}^{m}\|\bm{a}_i\|_1-C)/m$ and $\lambda_r=\max_i\|\bm{a}_i\|_1$.

By Lemma \ref{lemma:KKT_conditin}, we have $\mathcal{B}(\lambda_r) = \{i \mid \|\bm{a}_i\|_1>\lambda_r\}=\emptyset$, so $s(\lambda_r)=\sum_{i\in\mathcal{B}^r}\mu_i(\lambda_r)-C=-C<0$ and $\lambda^*<\lambda_r$.
    On the other hand, we can rewrite \eqref{eq:primal_stationary} to have

We denote $\{\lambda^k\}$ as the sequence generated by Algorithm \ref{alg:PDN} in this section. 
The next result provides the main property of $\{\lambda^k\}$.

$\{\lambda^k\}$ is monotonically non-decreasing and converges.
Moreover, $\lim_{k\to\infty}\lambda^k\leq\lambda^*$.

We prove this conclusion by induction, $\lambda^0\leq\lambda^*$ by Lemma \ref{lemma:range_of_lambda}, we now prove that if $\lambda^k\leq\lambda^*$,  then $\lambda^{k+1}\leq\lambda^*$.  First of all, this is trivially true if $\sum_{i\in\mathcal{B}^k}\mu_i^{k+1}-C \le 0$ by the update 
    of \eqref{eq:newton_step}.  
    Now we consider the case that $\sum_{i\in\mathcal{B}^k}\mu_i^{k+1}-C > 0$.  It follows from Lemma \ref{lemma:mu_will_update} that  $\sum_{i\in\mathcal{B}^k}\mu_i^{k+1}-C\leq\sum_{i\in\mathcal{B}^k}\mu_i(\lambda^{k})-C=s(\lambda^k)$, then the update of $\lambda^k$ implies

where $s'(\lambda^k)\in\partial s(\lambda^k)$.
    Similar to the proof of Lemma \ref{lemma:mu_will_update},
    it holds that $s(\tilde\lambda^{k+1})\geq s(\lambda^{k })+s'(\lambda^{k })(\tilde\lambda^{k+1}-\lambda^k)=0$,
    therefore $\tilde\lambda^{k+1}\leq\lambda^*$ by the fact that $s(\lambda)$ is monotonically decreasing, yielding $\lambda^{k+1} \leq\lambda^*$ by \eqref{eq:dual_update_new_form}. 
 By induction, $\{\lambda^k\}$ is bounded above by $\lambda^*$.  Therefore $\{\lambda^k\}$ converges and $\lim_{k\to\infty}\lambda^k\leq\lambda^*$.
The sequence $\{\lambda^k\}$ converges to $\lambda^*$. That is, $\lim_{k\to\infty}\lambda^k=\lambda^*$.

We prove this statement by contradiction. We suppose that there exists $\sigma > 0$ such that $\bar\lambda := \lim_{k\to\infty}\lambda^k <\lambda^*-\sigma$.  
    Since $s(\lambda)$ is a continuous linear function, $s(\bar\lambda)>L\epsilon$ for some $L>0$.
    However, note that we have $\lambda^k=\bar{\lambda}$ in \eqref{eq:dual_update_new_form} by Lemma \ref{lemma:mu_will_update} ($\lambda^j=\bar\lambda$ for $j=k+1,\dots,...$).
    However, note that $\alpha^k\geq1/m$, we have $\bar\lambda+s(\bar\lambda)/\alpha^k>\bar\lambda+L\sigma/m$, which contradicts with the fact that $\bar\lambda$ is the limit of $\{\lambda^k\}$.
    Therefore, we have $\lim_{k\to\infty}\lambda^k=\lambda^*$.

A direct consequence of Theorem \ref{lemma:convergence_lambda} and Lemma \ref{lemma:mu_will_update} is that our proposed algorithm produces a sequence that converges to the optimal primal-dual pair $(\boldsymbol\mu^*,\lambda^*)$.
The sequence $(\boldsymbol\mu^k, \lambda^k)$ generated by Algorithm \ref{alg:PDN} converges to the KKT point of problem \eqref{eq:opt_on_mu}.
Let $q(\lambda) = \min_{\boldsymbol\mu}\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol\mu,\lambda)$, then $q(\lambda)$ is a piecewise-quadratic, concave and first order differentiable function.

We have proved that $\{\lambda^k\}$ converges to $\lambda^*$. 
    From Lemma \ref{lemma:dual_quadratic}, the dual objective $q(\lambda)$ is a piecewise-quadratic function. 
    When $|\lambda^k-\lambda^*|<\sigma$ for sufficiently small $\sigma>0$, meaning that $\lambda^k$ and $\lambda^*$ lie in the same interval. 
    Then it can be seen from the proof Lemma \ref{lemma:mu_will_update} that $\boldsymbol\mu^{k+1}=\boldsymbol\mu^*$.
    Then equation holds in \eqref{eq:dual_update_new_form} and the update is exactly the Newton step at $\lambda^k$. 
    $q(\lambda)$ is a quadratic function in this interval means the Newton step finds the optimum in one step, that is, $\lambda^{k+1}=\lambda^*$.
    This proves the finite step convergence.

Though we do not give the explicit number of iteration of our algorithm as \cite{pmlr-v119-chu20b, Bejar19}. Our algorithm will terminates in at most $n^2m$ itrations in worst case. This is because if we fix $\lambda^k$ and solves $\mu_i(\lambda^k)$ exactly (which takes at most $n$ steps from Lemma \ref{lemma:mu_will_update}), then our algorithm reduces to Algorithm 4 of \cite{Bejar19} and converges in at most $mn$ iterations.

Similar to \cite{pmlr-v119-chu20b}, we first choose various factor $\gamma\in[0,1]$ such that the radius of the $\ell_{1,\infty}$ norm ball is given by $C=\gamma\|\mathbf{A}\|_{1,\infty}$.
SBM, SNA, PDN are then invoked to solve problem \eqref{problem:original_problem}, while NRFM\cite{Chau19}

is not used for speed comparison since its performance was reported in \cite{pmlr-v119-chu20b} to be outperformed by SNA.
The CPU time is recorded for each algorithm and all experiment results are the average of $10$ trials. 
The terminal criterion for PDN is set as

and  $\epsilon$ is chosen as $10^{-10}$.
and $\lambda^0$ is set as $\lambda_l$ for PDN defined in Lemma \ref{lemma:range_of_lambda}, and $0$ for SNA. 
The error is measured by $|\|\mathbf{W}^*\|_{1,\infty} -C|$. 
Due to the memory limitation, the size of the matrix $\mathbf{A}$ is set as $m=10000$ and $n=10000$ and entries of $\mathbf{A}$ are sampled from the normal distribution.
We record the error and time (in seconds) of three algorithms, and compute the speedup with respect to SBM.
The results are shown in Table \ref{table:speed_comparison}.

We also explore the relationship between the dimension and running time as did in  \cite{pmlr-v119-chu20b}. 
We set $m\in\{1000,2000,\dots,10000\}$ and generate the data matrix $\mathbf{A}\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times m}$ from the normal distribution. 
We set $\gamma=0.1$, other configurations are the same as the previous.
The results are shown in Fig \ref{fig:time_vs_dimension}. 
As we can see, as the dimension increases, our algorithm outweighs SBM and SNA in all cases, and the advantage seems significant especially in large-scale cases.

We then investigate the iterate information of PDN, NRFM and SNA. We record the history information of three algorithms for finding the projection of a given matrix $\mathbf{A}\in\mathbb{R}^{1000\times 1000}$ onto the $\ell_{1,\infty}$ norm ball with radius $C=0.5\|\mathbf{A}\|_{1,\infty}$. SBM is not an iterative algorithm, thus we did not plot its iterate information here.
The results are shown in Fig \ref{fig:iteration_information}.

As is can be seen from the Fig \ref{fig:iteration_information}, three algorithms take roughly the same iterations until converges.
However, if we plot the primal error and dual error againt the time, it can be seen that our proposed algorithm overwhelms NRFM and SNA, this is because our algorithm has lower computational complexity compared with two other algorithms at each iteration, 
meanwhile it explains the excellent performance of our proposed algorithm.
The iterate of $\lambda^k$ also validates the theoretical results of Lemma \ref{lemma:lower_bound_lambda}. That is, the sequence $\{\lambda^k\}$ produced by our algorithm is always an lower bound of that produced by NRFM.

In this subsection, we explore the performance of our proposed algorithm in the multi-task lasso \cite{KimSD10} problems, which are typically researched in feature selection. Given data matrices $\mathbf{X}_j\in\mathbb{R}^{n_i\times d}$ of the task $j$ for $j=1,\dots,n$. Multi-task Lasso aims to find a matrix $\mathbf{W}=[\bm{w}_1;\dots;\bm{w}_n]\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times n}$, such that the rows of $\mathbf{W}$ measures the importance of the corresponding feature among all tasks. The problem can be written in the form

We use the GPA defined in \eqref{eq:projection_step} to solve this problem, the stepsize $\gamma^k$ is set as $1/\max_i\lambda_{\max}(\mathbf{X}_i\mathbf{X}_i^T)$, where $\lambda_{\max}(\mathbf{A})$ represents the maximum eigenvalue of the matrix $\mathbf{A}$. The projection gradient method is stopped when $\|\mathbf{W}^{k+1}-\mathbf{W}^k\|_F^2\leq 10^{-5}$ or the maximum iterations ($1000$ iterations for synthetic data and $10000$ iterations for school dataset) is reached, the setup for the projection algorithms are the same as the previous subsection.

We first randomly generate data and compare the performance of three algorithms on the synthetic data. 
Similar to  \cite{Sra11}, we configure the number of features $d$, the number of tasks $n$ and the samples for each task $m$. 
The data matrix $\mathbf{X}_i\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times d}$ for $i=1,\dots,n$ are then sampled from the normal distribution. The radius is set as $C=0.1n$. 
Table \ref{table:multi_lasso_synthetic} summarizes four different configurations, which varies from small, medium, large, and very large-scale data.

The results are shown in Table \ref{table:multi_lasso_synthetic}. The results show that PDN is around $2$ times faster than SNA on large and very large dimension dataset, which illustrates the superior performance of PDN.

\cite{pmlr-v119-chu20b}. 
The School dataset consists of  $\sum_{i}n_i=15362$ test scores of the students from $n=139$ schools, each student is described with $d=27$ features. 
We compare the running time of the projection step in different radius $C$. 
The results are shown in Table \ref{table:dataset_school}.
Since the dimension of the problem is small, the proposed PDN and SNA are not competitive as the synthetic case. 
As the dimension of the problem becomes higher, the acceleration effect will become more clear.

In this paper, we propose a primal-dual Newton method for efficiently computing the projection onto the $\ell_{1, \infty}$ norm ball. 
The algorithm alternatively updates the primal and dual variable with the Newton steps, it avoids the computation of the subproblems of projection onto the $\ell_1$ ball, which significantly reduces the computational cost.
A finite step convergence result is derived.
Experiments on synthetic and real-world data exhibits the superior performance of our proposed algorithm especially on large dataset.

One of the most popular methods for handling convex constraint optimization problems is the gradient projection algorithm (GPA) \cite{Rosen60,Birgi00},
which is  often considered as a scalable solver for large-scale optimization problems, the update of the GPA applied to \eqref{problem:opt_on_ball} is defined as

The difference between our method and PDG is that we update the primal and dual variables all by the Newton steps. Usually, the computational cost for the Newton steps is not trivially, since it needs to evaluate the second-order derivative and then solve the Newton equation. However, in our case, we can exploit the problem-inherent structure to derive the closed-form solution of the Newton steps. We show that this method terminates in finite steps --- a novel feature that the general PDG does not possess.

In Section \ref{sec:problem_formulation}, we state the problem formulation and the optimality conditions.
In Section \ref{sec:algorithm}, we exploit the property of the projection and describe the proposed algorithm in detail. 
We provide the convergence analysis in Section \ref{sec:convergence_analysis}.
Numerical experiments are presented in Section \ref{sec:experiment_results}.
Concluding remarks are provided in Section \ref{sec:conclusion}.

In this section, we validate the performance of our proposed algorithm.
All experiments are done in a 2.40GHz, 8 core precision tower with Intel E5 processor and using MATLAB2021b. 
We denote our algorithm as PDN and the state-of-the-art algorithm proposed by  \cite{pmlr-v119-chu20b} SNA

is also the same as  used in \cite{pmlr-v119-chu20b}. 
SBM, SNA are re-implemented with MATLAB for consistency (code of SBM written by the author is used directly, SNA and NRFM are re-implemented by MATLAB for fairness). 
Our implementation will be released publicly on the Github for reproducing the results.

The results in Table \ref{table:speed_comparison} show that our proposed algorithm achieves better performance compared to SNA while maintaining the comparable accuracy of the optimal solution.
When the radius becomes small, PDN tends to be even faster.
We also test our algorithm on the School dataset
